program: Business English — Executive Communication for International Leadership
hub_subtitle: Business English Executivo para Reuniões Internacionais e Liderança

lesson.menu_desc: Weighing hypothetical strategy and negotiation scenarios -- the second conditional (if + past simple, would + base) -- 31 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 9 -- The What-If Room -- Weighing Hypotheticals with the Second Conditional
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Izabel Cristina de Pieri | Aula 9 -- The What-If Room

listenings.text: Hi Izabel, this is Marcus, your M and A advisor, thinking out loud before Thursday. Let me play a what-if with you. Imagine a much larger group offered to acquire AVRT next quarter. What would you do? If I were you, I would not answer too fast. If you sold now, you would lock in a good price, but you would lose your leverage over the roadmap. Here is another scenario. Suppose a giant competitor decided to undercut you on price. You would not have to follow them down. If your lead time and your quality stayed ahead, you would still win the serious buyers. And the big contract they mentioned? If you took it, it would stretch your capacity and create a bottleneck in production, so I would treat the timeline as a bargaining chip. If they made one real concession on delivery, I would sign. If they did not, I would walk away. Nothing here is certain. It is all hypothetical. But if we mapped every worst-case scenario now, we would sleep better on Thursday. Call me.

Good morning, Izabel. It is Vivian, your strategy consultant, with a few hypotheticals before the call. I want you to think in what-if today, not in what-will. First, the big contract. If AVRT accepted it, the overhead would rise sharply, and a second plant would only be feasible if the volume held for two full years. So my advice is simple. If I had to choose, I would hedge. I would take half the volume and keep the rest of your capacity free. Second, the price war. If a rival undercut us tomorrow, we would not panic. If we cut our price to match, our margin would collapse, so we would compete on quality instead. Every choice here is a trade-off. If we chased growth at any cost, we would risk the core business. But if we offered the buyer a small incentive, a faster delivery, say, we would win the deal without touching the price. Remember, these are hypotheticals. If the numbers were different, my advice would change. Talk soon.
